title: Fatigue, dark urine, and a peaked-T-wave EKG after a new sulfa antibiotic

chart.oneLiner: 28M of Mediterranean descent with HIV on antiretrovirals and recently switched PCP prophylaxis presents with two days of fatigue, jaundice, and cola-colored urine that began shortly after starting trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole for a urinary tract infection; he is tachycardic with a peaked-T-wave EKG and a rising creatinine.

opening: I've felt wiped out and short of breath for a couple of days, my eyes look a bit yellow, and my urine has turned almost like cola.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 28-year-old man of Mediterranean (Greek) descent, mildly jaundiced and tired-appearing. Cooperative, mildly anxious about the dark urine. Speaks in lay terms.

HPI: Acute onset over 48 hours of fatigue, dyspnea on exertion, and lightheadedness. Noticed scleral yellowing and dark cola-colored urine, with the volume of urine seeming to drop over the last day. Symptoms started two to three days after beginning trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (a sulfa antibiotic) for a urinary tract infection (only volunteer the antibiotic if asked about recent medications/new drugs). Mild back/flank discomfort with the dark urine. NO frank red blood in urine, NO dysuria now, NO black/bloody stools, NO trauma, NO chest pain or palpitations. Reports a similar but milder episode years ago after eating fava beans, which he otherwise avoids (only if asked about prior episodes or diet/food triggers).
PMH: HIV diagnosed 3 years ago, well controlled on antiretroviral therapy with an undetectable viral load and a CD4 around 350 (only if asked about medical conditions/HIV). Episode of neonatal jaundice per his mother (only if asked). A maternal uncle has 'a blood problem with certain drugs' (family history, only if asked).
MEDS (reveal the list only when asked what medicines he takes / any new medicines): (1) bictegravir/emtricitabine/tenofovir alafenamide once daily for HIV — long-standing, no recent change; (2) trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole started 2-3 days ago for a UTI (the NEW drug); (3) his PCP-prophylaxis was switched a few weeks ago from atovaquone to DAPSONE because of cost — he has been taking dapsone daily (only if specifically asked about prophylaxis or all medications, including 'anything for prevention'); (4) occasional over-the-counter ibuprofen. ALLERGIES: none known. SOCIAL: occasional alcohol; non-smoker; no IV drugs; recently ate at a relative's home where fava beans were served (only if asked about diet). FAMILY: maternal uncle with a drug-related blood problem; mother recalls his neonatal jaundice.
ROS: as above; denies fevers, confusion, headache, diarrhea, easy bruising, nosebleeds, or melena.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: tired-appearing, mildly jaundiced, not in distress.
- Vitals: BP 118/72, HR 110, RR 20, 37.3°C, SpO2 98% RA.
- HEENT: scleral icterus; conjunctival pallor.
- Abdomen: soft; spleen tip only minimally palpable / generally not enlarged; no marked tenderness; no flank mass.
- Cardiac: tachycardic, regular, soft systolic flow murmur; no rub.
- Skin: no petechiae, no purpura, no rash; no track marks. No focal neuro deficit.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the new antibiotic, the dapsone prophylaxis, the fava bean exposure, the neonatal jaundice, the HIV, or the family history unless asked about recent medications, prophylaxis/preventive medicines, diet/food, prior episodes, medical history, or family history respectively.
- The dapsone is easy to miss: only disclose it if the student asks specifically about ALL medications, preventive/prophylactic medicines, or recent medication changes.
- The spleen finding is revealed only if the abdomen/spleen is examined.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.
- Use lay language; avoid medical jargon and textbook phrasing.

labs.CBC: Hgb 7.9, MCV 89 (normocytic), WBC 8.6 (normal), Plt 248 (NORMAL platelets — important to exclude TTP/HUS). No leukoerythroblastic picture.
labs.Reticulocyte: Reticulocyte count ELEVATED (~7%) — an appropriate marrow response to hemolysis.
labs.Smear: Bite cells and blister cells; with supravital (crystal violet) stain Heinz bodies present; polychromasia; NO schistocytes (argues against a microangiopathic process).
labs.Hemolysis: LDH HIGH, indirect (unconjugated) bilirubin HIGH (total bili 4.2 / direct 0.6), haptoglobin LOW, plasma free hemoglobin present, urine dipstick positive for blood with NO red cells on micro (pigment = hemoglobinuria) — an intravascular hemolytic pattern.
labs.BMP: Na 138, K 6.1 (HIGH), Cl 103, HCO3 19 → anion gap = 138 − (103 + 19) = 16 (mildly elevated); BUN 38, Cr 2.1 (baseline ~0.9) — acute kidney injury from pigment (hemoglobinuria) nephropathy; the hyperkalemia reflects both the AKI and potassium released by intravascular red-cell lysis.
labs.Coombs: Direct antiglobulin (Coombs) test NEGATIVE for both IgG and C3 — argues AGAINST drug-induced IMMUNE (warm-autoimmune-type) hemolysis from the sulfa.
labs.G6PD: G6PD enzyme activity LOW once the acute episode/reticulocytosis settles (may be falsely NORMAL during the acute crisis because young reticulocytes are enzyme-replete — repeat in 2–3 months).

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Sinus tachycardia with tall, narrow, symmetrically PEAKED T waves (best seen in the precordial leads) and early PR prolongation — changes of hyperkalemia (K 6.1) from acute kidney injury and intravascular hemolysis; no acute ischemic ST depression or convex ST elevation.
ekg.verify: Source a study matching: peaked/tented T waves of hyperkalemia on a background of sinus tachycardia, without QRS widening to a sine-wave. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.
ekg.attribution: LITFL ECG Library (litfl.com) — © Life in the Fast Lane, CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. Educational/not-for-profit use, credit litfl.com; skip any image labelled third-party.
ekg.imageDescription: 12-lead showing sinus tachycardia with tall, tented, peaked T waves of hyperkalemia
ekg.clinicalVignette: A young man with brisk intravascular hemolysis and a rising creatinine has a 12-lead obtained; the tracing shows tall, tented T waves.

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: No acute cardiopulmonary process; clear lungs with a normal cardiac silhouette and no pulmonary edema — argues against a cardiopulmonary cause of the dyspnea, which is from the anemia.
cxr.verify: Source a study matching: normal chest radiograph with no acute process. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.
cxr.clinicalVignette: A 28-year-old man with weakness and dark urine has a chest film obtained to exclude a cardiopulmonary cause of his dyspnea.

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential for this acute anemia (note your leading diagnosis). This man started a sulfa antibiotic days before the dark urine, so explicitly weigh oxidative versus immune drug-related hemolysis. Also NAME THREE distinct mechanisms/triggers of Coombs-negative oxidative hemolysis in someone like him.
steps.rubric: Acute oxidative hemolytic anemia (G6PD deficiency) triggered by a new oxidant drug/fava beans (leading,6). Drug-induced IMMUNE (autoimmune-type) hemolysis from the sulfa antibiotic — the genuine competing diagnosis to resolve with a Coombs test (CANT-MISS to distinguish,5). Microangiopathic hemolysis — TTP/HUS — must be excluded (CANT-MISS,4). Autoimmune hemolytic anemia (warm/cold) (core,2). Hemoglobinuria from other causes (rhabdomyolysis, transfusion reaction) (core,1). Names hemolysis and the imperative to look for schistocytes/thrombocytopenia to exclude TTP/HUS (core,3). Names THREE distinct oxidant mechanisms/triggers — e.g. sulfa antibiotics (TMP-SMX), dapsone, fava beans (favism), other oxidants such as primaquine/nitrofurantoin/rasburicase/naphthalene (core,4). PENALTY: anchoring on a single drug mechanism without sending a Coombs to separate immune from oxidative hemolysis (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Acute OXIDATIVE hemolytic anemia from G6PD deficiency, triggered by a new oxidant exposure (the sulfa antibiotic and/or dapsone, layered on recent fava beans). The genuine competing diagnosis given the temporal link to the sulfa is DRUG-INDUCED IMMUNE hemolytic anemia (a Coombs-positive, autoimmune-type reaction to the drug) — the discriminating test is the direct antiglobulin (Coombs) test. Must-not-miss: Microangiopathic hemolysis (TTP/HUS) — look for schistocytes, thrombocytopenia, and renal/neuro involvement. Also reasonable: warm/cold autoimmune hemolytic anemia; hemoglobinuria from rhabdomyolysis or a transfusion reaction. Three distinct mechanisms/triggers of Coombs-negative oxidative hemolysis here: (1) sulfonamide antibiotics (TMP-SMX); (2) dapsone (his switched PCP prophylaxis); (3) fava beans / favism — with other classic oxidants being primaquine, nitrofurantoin, rasburicase, and naphthalene.

criticalActions.item: Acute oxidative hemolytic anemia — G6PD deficiency triggered by a new oxidant drug/fava beans
Drug-induced IMMUNE hemolysis from the sulfa antibiotic — the competing diagnosis resolved by a Coombs test

coreActions.item: Hemoglobinuria from other causes (rhabdomyolysis, transfusion reaction)
Names hemolysis and the imperative to look for schistocytes/thrombocytopenia to exclude TTP/HUS
Names THREE distinct oxidant mechanisms/triggers (e.g. sulfa antibiotics, dapsone, fava beans, primaquine/nitrofurantoin/rasburicase)

penalties.item: Anchors on a single drug mechanism without sending a Coombs to separate immune from oxidative hemolysis

mustNotMiss: Drug-induced IMMUNE hemolysis from the sulfa antibiotic
acceptableAlternatives: Hemoglobinuria from other causes (rhabdomyolysis, transfusion reaction)

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial workup do you want to order?
steps.rubric: CBC + reticulocyte count (CANT-MISS,3). Peripheral smear — bite/blister cells, Heinz bodies vs schistocytes (CANT-MISS,4). Direct antiglobulin (Coombs) test to separate oxidative from immune drug hemolysis — expected NEGATIVE (CANT-MISS,3). BMP for potassium/renal function (the AKI + hyperkalemia drive the EKG) (2). Hemolysis labs — LDH, haptoglobin, indirect bilirubin, urine hemoglobin (1). G6PD enzyme assay (confirmatory, repeat after the acute episode) (1). EKG and CXR (1). PENALTY: relying on a single normal G6PD level drawn during the acute crisis to exclude the diagnosis (-2); failing to send a BMP/potassium in a brisk intravascular hemolysis with dark urine (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: CBC; Reticulocyte count; Peripheral smear (bite/blister cells, Heinz bodies vs schistocytes); Direct antiglobulin (Coombs) test to separate oxidative from immune drug hemolysis; BMP (the rising creatinine and hyperkalemia from AKI/cell lysis are what produce the peaked-T-wave EKG); Hemolysis labs (LDH, haptoglobin, indirect bilirubin, urine hemoglobin); G6PD enzyme assay (confirmatory — repeat 2–3 months after recovery as it can be falsely normal during reticulocytosis); EKG; CXR.

criticalActions.item: Peripheral smear — bite/blister cells, Heinz bodies vs schistocytes

coreActions.item: BMP for potassium and renal function
Hemolysis labs (LDH, haptoglobin, indirect bilirubin, urine hemoglobin)
G6PD enzyme assay (repeat after acute episode)

penalties.item: relying on a single normal G6PD level drawn during the acute crisis to exclude the diagnosis
failing to send a BMP/potassium in brisk intravascular hemolysis with dark urine

requiredTests: Peripheral smear — bite/blister cells, Heinz bodies vs schistocytes
optionalTests: BMP for potassium and renal function
G6PD enzyme assay (repeat after acute episode)
contraindicatedTests: relying on a single normal G6PD level drawn during the acute crisis to exclude the diagnosis
orderSensitiveActions: relying on a single normal G6PD level drawn during the acute crisis to exclude the diagnosis

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is THIS patient's own 12-lead EKG, obtained because of his dark urine and rising creatinine. Describe your findings and their relevance to his management.
steps.rubric: Findings = sinus tachycardia with tall, narrow, symmetrically PEAKED/tented T waves of HYPERKALEMIA (K 6.1) from the AKI and intravascular hemolysis, without sine-wave QRS widening; no acute ischemia. CREDIT: identifies the peaked/tented T waves as hyperkalemia (7); links the hyperkalemia to the pigment-nephropathy AKI and potassium released by red-cell lysis, and notes there is not yet QRS widening (5); systematic read with rate/rhythm and pertinent negatives (3). PENALTY: calling the tracing normal/ischemic or missing the peaked T waves as hyperkalemia (-3).
keyFindings: tall, narrow, symmetrically peaked/tented T waves of hyperkalemia
sinus tachycardia; no QRS sine-wave widening yet; no acute ischemia
criticalFindings: Sinus tachycardia with peaked/tented T waves of hyperkalemia (K 6.1) from AKI and hemolysis.
commonMistakes: calling the tracing normal or over-reading the T waves as ischemia
missing that the peaked T waves signal a dangerous, treatable hyperkalemia
steps.idealAnswer: Sinus tachycardia with tall, narrow, symmetrically peaked (tented) T waves, best seen in the precordial leads, with early PR prolongation — the EKG signature of HYPERKALEMIA (here K 6.1). There is no QRS sine-wave widening yet and no acute ischemic ST change. This is his own potassium of 6.1, driven by the acute kidney injury from pigment (hemoglobinuria) nephropathy and by potassium released from the intravascular red-cell lysis. It is directly relevant: he needs urgent potassium management (cardiac membrane stabilization with calcium if there are EKG changes, insulin/glucose, and treatment of the AKI/hemolysis), and any further oxidant exposure or worsening hemolysis could push the potassium higher.

criticalActions: Sinus tachycardia with peaked/tented T waves of hyperkalemia (K 6.1) from AKI and hemolysis.
coreActions: tall, narrow, symmetrically peaked/tented T waves of hyperkalemia
sinus tachycardia; no QRS sine-wave widening yet; no acute ischemia

penalties.label: calls the study normal/ischemic or misses the hyperkalemic peaked T waves

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = no acute process. CREDIT: identifies a normal film (6); notes it helps exclude an alternate cause of dyspnea — pneumonia, effusion, pulmonary edema — leaving the anemia as the explanation (4). PENALTY: inventing pathology (-2).
keyFindings: notes it excludes pneumonia/effusion/edema as a cause of dyspnea
commonMistakes: inventing an infiltrate or effusion that is not present
steps.idealAnswer: No acute cardiopulmonary process; clear lungs and a normal cardiac silhouette with no pulmonary edema. This excludes pneumonia, effusion, or heart failure as the cause of his dyspnea, leaving the anemia as the explanation.

coreActions: notes it excludes pneumonia/effusion/edema as a cause of dyspnea

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: You now have: reticulocytosis, low haptoglobin, high LDH/indirect bilirubin, bite cells and Heinz bodies, NO schistocytes, normal platelets, a NEGATIVE Coombs, an oxidant drug/fava-bean exposure, AND a BMP showing K 6.1 with Cr 2.1 plus peaked T waves on EKG. Commit to a diagnosis, name the discriminating test, state what you ruled out, and explain the second active problem the labs reveal.
steps.rubric: G6PD deficiency with acute OXIDATIVE intravascular hemolysis (5). Names the NEGATIVE Coombs as the discriminator that excludes drug-induced IMMUNE/autoimmune hemolysis, and cites bite cells/Heinz bodies plus the oxidant trigger to favor oxidative G6PD hemolysis; rules out TTP/HUS via absent schistocytes, normal platelets (3). Recognizes the SECOND concurrent problem — hyperkalemia and AKI from pigment (hemoglobinuria) nephropathy and cell lysis, shown by the peaked-T-wave EKG — as a separate, urgent issue, and acknowledges G6PD can be falsely normal during the crisis so it must be repeated after recovery (2).
steps.idealAnswer: G6PD deficiency causing acute OXIDATIVE, intravascular, drug/fava-bean-triggered hemolysis. The discriminating test is the direct antiglobulin (Coombs) test: it is NEGATIVE, which excludes the competing drug-induced IMMUNE hemolysis and autoimmune hemolysis; bite cells and Heinz bodies with hemolysis markers after an oxidant exposure clinch the oxidative mechanism. TTP/HUS is excluded by the absence of schistocytes and the normal platelet count. The labs reveal a SECOND, concurrent and urgent problem: acute kidney injury with hyperkalemia (K 6.1, Cr 2.1) from pigment (hemoglobinuria) nephropathy and potassium released by red-cell lysis — this is what produces the peaked T waves on his EKG and must be treated in parallel. Note that the G6PD assay can be falsely normal during the acute reticulocytosis, so it should be repeated 2–3 months after recovery to confirm.
mustNotMiss: Drug-induced IMMUNE hemolysis from the sulfa antibiotic

steps.label: Management
steps.prompt: What is your management plan, including how you will handle his potassium and his disposition/safe-discharge plan?
steps.rubric: Per MGH hemolytic anemia + hyperkalemia: immediately STOP/avoid the offending oxidants — the sulfa antibiotic AND the dapsone prophylaxis — and counsel on triggers (CANT-MISS,6); treat the HYPERKALEMIA driving the peaked-T-wave EKG — calcium for membrane stabilization given EKG changes, insulin/glucose +/- albuterol to shift, and address the underlying AKI/hemolysis (CANT-MISS,5); supportive care with IV hydration to protect the kidneys from hemoglobinuria and serial Hgb/renal/potassium monitoring (3); transfuse if anemia is severe/symptomatic (2); folate supplementation (1); patient education with a written oxidant avoid-list and screen at-risk family (2); ADMIT given active hemolysis, AKI, and hyperkalemia — do NOT discharge until the potassium and creatinine are improving, the offending drugs are stopped and reconciled, a non-oxidant PCP-prophylaxis (e.g. atovaquone) is substituted with hematology/HIV follow-up arranged (CANT-MISS safe disposition,4); confirm with a repeat G6PD assay after recovery (bonus,2). PENALTY: continuing/re-prescribing the sulfa or dapsone or giving another oxidant (nitrofurantoin, primaquine, rasburicase) (-3); discharging home with an unstable potassium/active hemolysis (-3); reflexively starting steroids as if this were autoimmune hemolysis (-1).
steps.idealAnswer: G6PD oxidative hemolysis with concurrent AKI and hyperkalemia. (1) Immediately discontinue and avoid BOTH offending oxidants — the sulfa antibiotic AND the dapsone prophylaxis — and remove other triggers (fava beans, naphthalene); reconcile the full med list. (2) Treat the hyperkalemia producing the peaked-T-wave EKG: IV calcium (gluconate/chloride) for cardiac membrane stabilization because there are EKG changes, then insulin with glucose +/- nebulized albuterol to shift potassium intracellularly, and treat the underlying driver (the AKI and ongoing hemolysis); recheck the EKG and potassium. (3) Supportive care: IV hydration to maintain urine output and protect against hemoglobinuria-related renal injury, with serial Hgb, creatinine, and potassium. (4) Transfuse packed red cells if the anemia is severe or symptomatic. (5) Folate supplementation. (6) Patient education with a written list of oxidant drugs/foods to avoid, and screen at-risk family members. (7) DISPOSITION/SAFE DISCHARGE: ADMIT — he has active intravascular hemolysis, AKI, and a dangerous potassium; do not discharge until the potassium and creatinine are trending down, hemolysis is settling, the offending drugs are stopped and clearly documented as adverse reactions, and his PCP prophylaxis is switched back to a non-oxidant agent (e.g. atovaquone). Arrange close hematology and HIV/ID follow-up and confirm with a repeat G6PD enzyme assay 2–3 months after recovery (it can be falsely normal during reticulocytosis). Most episodes are self-limited as the older RBCs are cleared.

criticalActions.item: Stop/avoid BOTH offending oxidants (the sulfa antibiotic AND dapsone) and counsel on triggers
Treat the hyperkalemia — calcium for EKG changes, insulin/glucose +/- albuterol, address AKI/hemolysis

coreActions.item: Supportive care — IV hydration to protect kidneys, serial Hgb/renal/potassium monitoring
Patient education with oxidant avoid-list; screen at-risk family
Safe disposition — ADMIT until K/Cr improving, oxidant drugs stopped/reconciled, non-oxidant PCP prophylaxis substituted, hematology/HIV follow-up arranged

bonusActions.item: Hematology consult; confirm with repeat G6PD assay after recovery

penalties.item: Continuing or re-prescribing the sulfa/dapsone / giving another oxidant
Discharging home with an unstable potassium / active hemolysis

requiredActions: Stop/avoid BOTH offending oxidants (the sulfa antibiotic AND dapsone) and counsel on triggers
Treat the hyperkalemia — calcium for EKG changes, insulin/glucose +/- albuterol, address AKI/hemolysis
optionalActions: Supportive care — IV hydration to protect kidneys, serial Hgb/renal/potassium monitoring
Patient education with oxidant avoid-list; screen at-risk family
Safe disposition — ADMIT until K/Cr improving, oxidant drugs stopped/reconciled, non-oxidant PCP prophylaxis substituted, hematology/HIV follow-up arranged
Hematology consult; confirm with repeat G6PD assay after recovery
unsafeActions: Continuing or re-prescribing the sulfa/dapsone / giving another oxidant
Discharging home with an unstable potassium / active hemolysis

physicalExamMappings.finding: HEENT: conjunctival pallor consistent with anemia.
Cardiac: tachycardic, regular, soft systolic flow murmur; no rub. No focal neuro deficit.
physicalExamMappings.normalIfNotPresent: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmur.

physicalExamMappings.finding: Skin: no petechiae, no purpura, no rash (pertinent negative against TTP/HUS).

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 28M of Mediterranean descent with HIV on antiretrovirals and a recently switched (cost-driven) dapsone PCP prophylaxis presents with two days of fatigue, jaundice, and cola-colored urine after starting trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole for a UTI. The diagnosis is G6PD deficiency with acute oxidative intravascular hemolysis, reached by eliciting the layered oxidant-trigger history, performing the targeted exam, and interpreting the directed workup (bite cells/Heinz bodies, NEGATIVE Coombs, hemolytic markers). The genuine competing diagnosis is drug-induced IMMUNE hemolysis from the sulfa, resolved by the negative Coombs; TTP/HUS is the must-not-miss excluded by normal platelets and absent schistocytes. A concurrent, urgent second problem — hyperkalemia (K 6.1) with AKI (Cr 2.1) from pigment nephropathy and cell lysis — produces the peaked-T-wave EKG and must be treated in parallel.
keyHistoryClues: New sulfa antibiotic (TMP-SMX) two to three days before symptoms
Recently switched PCP prophylaxis to DAPSONE — a second oxidant that is easy to miss
Fava bean ingestion and a prior milder self-limited episode
Mediterranean ancestry, neonatal jaundice, and X-linked family history
Cola-colored urine without frank hematuria (intravascular hemolysis/hemoglobinuria)
keyPhysicalExamFindings: HEENT: conjunctival pallor consistent with anemia.
Cardiac: tachycardic, regular, soft systolic flow murmur; no rub. No focal neuro deficit.
Skin: no petechiae, no purpura, no rash (pertinent negative against TTP/HUS).
Abdomen: soft; spleen tip only minimally palpable / generally not enlarged; no marked tenderness.
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals.

Elicit the focused history, drawing out BOTH oxidant drugs (the new sulfa antibiotic and the switched dapsone prophylaxis), the fava bean trigger, ancestry, HIV status, and family history only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal jaundice, pallor, and the pertinent-negative absence of petechiae/purpura.

Build a differential with a real fork — oxidative G6PD hemolysis versus drug-induced IMMUNE hemolysis from the sulfa — while explicitly excluding must-not-miss TTP/HUS, and name three oxidant mechanisms.

Order a focused workup (CBC + retic, smear for bite cells/Heinz bodies, Coombs, BMP for potassium/renal function, G6PD assay), interpret the patient's own hyperkalemic peaked-T-wave EKG and the normal CXR, and recognize G6PD can be falsely normal during the crisis.

Commit to G6PD deficiency hemolysis, recognize the concurrent AKI/hyperkalemia, and manage by stopping BOTH oxidant drugs, treating the hyperkalemia, supportive hydration, selective transfusion, education, a safe-discharge/disposition plan, and hematology/HIV follow-up.
commonPitfalls: Anchoring on the sulfa as immune hemolysis without sending a Coombs, or missing the dapsone as a second oxidant
Continuing or re-prescribing the oxidant drug / giving another oxidant
Missing the hyperkalemia/AKI behind the peaked-T-wave EKG and discharging with an unstable potassium
Relying on a single normal G6PD level drawn during the acute crisis to exclude the diagnosis
Reflexively starting steroids as if this were autoimmune hemolysis
managementPearls: Stop BOTH offending oxidants (the sulfa antibiotic and dapsone) and counsel on triggers
Treat the hyperkalemia driving the peaked T waves — calcium for EKG changes, then insulin/glucose, and fix the AKI/hemolysis
Supportive hydration protects against hemoglobinuria-related renal injury
Safe discharge means admitting until K/Cr improve, drugs are reconciled, and non-oxidant prophylaxis is substituted
Repeat the G6PD assay 2–3 months after recovery to confirm
criticalWorkupItems: Peripheral smear — bite/blister cells, Heinz bodies vs schistocytes
criticalInterpretationItems: Sinus tachycardia with peaked/tented T waves of hyperkalemia (K 6.1) from AKI and hemolysis.
No acute cardiopulmonary process (excludes pneumonia/effusion/edema as a cause of dyspnea).
criticalManagementItems: Stop/avoid BOTH offending oxidants (the sulfa antibiotic AND dapsone) and counsel on triggers
Treat the hyperkalemia — calcium for EKG changes, insulin/glucose +/- albuterol, address AKI/hemolysis
keyImageInterpretationFindings: Sinus tachycardia with peaked/tented T waves of hyperkalemia (K 6.1).
criticalImageInterpretationFindings: Sinus tachycardia with peaked/tented T waves of hyperkalemia (K 6.1) from AKI and hemolysis.
imageInterpretationPearls: The patient's own EKG is part of the puzzle — peaked, tented T waves on a hemolysis/AKI patient are hyperkalemia and demand action, not just description.
Read every study systematically before committing; state pertinent negatives, not just positives.
A normal chest film is itself an interpretation task — name what is excluded (pneumonia, effusion, edema) and the remaining explanation (anemia).

items.item: Shows empathy for the patient's concern
Asks permission before sensitive questions (HIV, medications)
Explains next steps in patient-friendly language
